content.text: LLM inference is one of the most expensive compute primitives in modern software — orders of magnitude more expensive per operation than a database query or an API call. In an agentic AI system, costs compound across multi-step reasoning chains: each tool call may trigger additional LLM calls, embedding lookups, and provider API charges. Without treating token usage and cost as
first-class operational signals
— measured, alerted on, and attributed to specific agents, tenants, and features — cost can grow 10x in a week before anyone notices.
What It Is

Treating token usage and cost as operational signals means applying the same engineering discipline to spend that you apply to latency and error rate. Concretely, it means: defining Prometheus metrics for token consumption and inferred cost; setting up dashboards that show cost per agent session, per tenant, and per feature; alerting on anomalous spend before it breaches a budget; and attributing costs to the parts of the system that drive them — so that cost optimisation decisions are data-driven rather than guesswork.
This is distinct from billing: billing is a monthly reconciliation with the LLM provider. Cost as an operational signal is a
real-time or near-real-time
measurement that allows on-call engineers to detect cost anomalies within minutes, and allows product teams to understand the cost structure of features before they ship at scale.
Why It Matters

LLM cost is non-linear with respect to usage. A new agent feature that seems cheap in testing — because it only runs in low-volume experiments — can become extremely expensive when traffic scales. Without real-time cost signals, you discover this in the monthly invoice rather than in a dashboard alert. The consequences are immediate: surprise budget overruns, rushed emergency optimisation work, or service throttling by the LLM provider when spending exceeds account limits.

Beyond pure spend control, cost signals reveal efficiency: the ratio of cost to value delivered. A system that costs $0.01 per successful agent session with high user satisfaction is well-tuned. A system that costs $1.00 per session is either using the wrong model, sending unnecessarily large prompts, or running too many reasoning hops. Only by measuring cost as a signal can you detect and act on these inefficiencies.
Benefits
Real-time cost visibility: detect runaway spending within minutes of a deployment, not at month-end.
Per-feature cost attribution enables data-driven decisions about which features are worth their LLM cost.
Budget burn rate alerts catch multi-tenant cost spikes before they affect the overall account.
Cost-quality correlation reveals whether spending more on inference actually improves outcomes.
Token efficiency ratios identify agents that use excessive prompt tokens relative to their output value.
Multi-model routing decisions can be validated: does switching from a frontier model to a smaller model save cost without degrading the error rate?
Trade-offs
Cost metrics require knowledge of provider pricing at collection time. Pricing changes require metric pipeline updates.
High-cardinality attribution (per agent, per tenant, per feature, per model) can inflate Prometheus metric cardinality. Design label sets carefully.

Cost signals are approximate at the point of collection. Cached tokens, batch discounts, and prompt caching credits complicate exact real-time accounting. Use provider-authoritative billing as the source of truth for financial reporting.
Correlating cost with quality signals requires that both are measured consistently and annotated with the same session or request ID.
How It Works

Every LLM API response includes token usage counts: input tokens, output tokens, and sometimes cache read/write tokens. At the point of receiving the API response, the instrumentation layer reads these counts, multiplies by the per-token price of the model used, and emits both raw token counters and a derived cost counter in USD (or the operational currency unit). These metrics flow into Prometheus and are visualised in Grafana, with alerting on budget burn rate anomalies.

attrs.alt: Cost signal flow diagram: LLM API call response feeds token metrics and cost metrics with attribution by agent, tenant, and feature, feeding alerting for budget burn rate and per-tenant spikes, and cost efficiency ratios

content.text: Cost Metric Definitions
Core Cost Metrics
cost_usd_total
: A counter accumulating total LLM inference cost in USD, labelled by agent_name, model, tenant_id, and token_type (input/output).
cost_per_session
: The total cost incurred within one agent session. Derived: sum(cost_usd_total) within the session span duration.
cost_per_successful_outcome
: Total cost divided by number of sessions with status=success. This is the efficiency metric that connects spend to value.
cost_per_hour
: rate(cost_usd_total[1h]) * 3600. Useful for budget burn rate dashboards and for extrapolating daily/monthly spend.
Token Usage Signals
llm_input_tokens_total
: Counter of input tokens sent to the LLM, labelled by model and agent.
llm_output_tokens_total
: Counter of output tokens received from the LLM.
llm_cache_read_tokens_total
: Tokens served from the provider's prompt cache (cheaper than full input tokens). Tracking this separately reveals caching effectiveness.
agent_prompt_length_tokens
: Histogram of prompt lengths per session. Rising p95 prompt length is an early indicator of growing system prompt bloat.
token_efficiency_ratio

: output_tokens / (input_tokens + output_tokens). A ratio near 0.5 means roughly equal input and output; ratios below 0.1 indicate very large prompts producing small responses — often a sign of prompt engineering waste.

"""cost_instrumentation.py

Instruments token usage and cost for an agentic AI session.
Calls the Prometheus metrics API to record all cost signals.
"""
from opentelemetry import metrics
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Literal

# Model pricing table (USD per token, as of authoring; update when provider changes)
_PRICING: dict[str, dict[str, float]] = {
    "claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022": {
        "input": 3.00 / 1_000_000,
        "output": 15.00 / 1_000_000,
        "cache_read": 0.30 / 1_000_000,
    },
    "claude-3-5-haiku-20241022": {
        "input": 0.80 / 1_000_000,
        "output": 4.00 / 1_000_000,
        "cache_read": 0.08 / 1_000_000,
    },
    "gpt-4o": {
        "input": 2.50 / 1_000_000,
        "output": 10.00 / 1_000_000,
        "cache_read": 1.25 / 1_000_000,
    },
}

meter = metrics.get_meter("agent.cost", version="1.0")

# Token counters
input_tokens_counter = meter.create_counter(
    "llm_input_tokens_total",
    description="Total LLM input tokens consumed",
)
output_tokens_counter = meter.create_counter(
    "llm_output_tokens_total",
    description="Total LLM output tokens generated",
)
cache_read_tokens_counter = meter.create_counter(
    "llm_cache_read_tokens_total",
    description="LLM tokens served from provider prompt cache",
)

# Cost counter (USD, approximated at collection time)
cost_usd_counter = meter.create_counter(
    "llm_cost_usd_total",
    unit="USD",
    description="Approximate LLM inference cost in USD",
)

# Prompt length histogram
prompt_length_histogram = meter.create_histogram(
    "agent_prompt_length_tokens",
    description="Prompt (input) token count per LLM call",
    unit="tokens",
)


@dataclass
class LLMUsage:
    model: str
    input_tokens: int
    output_tokens: int
    cache_read_tokens: int = 0
    agent_name: str = "unknown"
    tenant_id: str = "unknown"
    feature_flag: str = "default"


def record_llm_usage(usage: LLMUsage) -> None:
    """Record token counts and derived cost from a single LLM API response."""
    attrs = {
        "model": usage.model,
        "agent_name": usage.agent_name,
        "tenant_id": usage.tenant_id,
        "feature_flag": usage.feature_flag,
    }

    # Raw token counters
    input_tokens_counter.add(usage.input_tokens, {**attrs, "token_type": "input"})
    output_tokens_counter.add(usage.output_tokens, {**attrs, "token_type": "output"})
    if usage.cache_read_tokens:
        cache_read_tokens_counter.add(
            usage.cache_read_tokens, {**attrs, "token_type": "cache_read"}
        )

    # Prompt length histogram (for trend analysis)
    prompt_length_histogram.record(usage.input_tokens, attrs)

    # Derived cost in USD
    pricing = _PRICING.get(usage.model)
    if pricing:
        cost = (
            usage.input_tokens * pricing["input"]
            + usage.output_tokens * pricing["output"]
            + usage.cache_read_tokens * pricing.get("cache_read", 0)
        )
        cost_usd_counter.add(cost, attrs)
    # If model not in pricing table, skip cost recording rather than guessing
Cost Attribution
Attribution is the process of assigning cost to the entity responsible for it. In an agentic AI platform, there are three important attribution dimensions:
By agent
: Which agent (support-agent, research-agent, coding-agent) is responsible for the cost? Agents with high cost per session are candidates for optimisation.
By tenant
: In multi-tenant systems, cost per tenant is essential for both billing and anomaly detection. A single tenant consuming 50x the average cost is either a power user or is stuck in an infinite loop.
By feature flag or model variant
: During A/B tests of different models or prompt strategies, cost attribution by feature flag lets you compare cost-efficiency across cohorts.

# Total cost per agent per hour
sum by (agent_name) (
  rate(llm_cost_usd_total[1h])
) * 3600

# Cost per session (approximation via ratio)
sum by (agent_name) (rate(llm_cost_usd_total[5m]))
/ sum by (agent_name) (rate(agent_sessions_total[5m]))

# Cost per successful outcome
sum by (agent_name) (rate(llm_cost_usd_total[5m]))
/ sum by (agent_name) (
    rate(agent_sessions_total{status="success"}[5m])
  )

# Token efficiency ratio (output tokens / total tokens)
sum(rate(llm_output_tokens_total[5m]))
/ (
  sum(rate(llm_input_tokens_total[5m]))
  + sum(rate(llm_output_tokens_total[5m]))
)

# Top 5 tenants by cost in last hour
topk(5,
  sum by (tenant_id) (
    increase(llm_cost_usd_total[1h])
  )
)

# Cost by feature flag — A/B cost comparison
sum by (feature_flag) (
  rate(llm_cost_usd_total[30m])
) * 3600
Alerting on Cost Anomalies
Budget Burn Rate Alerts
The most actionable cost alert is a
burn rate alert

: detecting when the current spending rate would exhaust the monthly budget before the end of the month. Use multi-window burn rates (1-hour fast burn, 6-hour slow burn) to catch both spikes and gradual drift.

# Prometheus alert rules for cost signals
groups:
  - name: agent-cost-alerts
    rules:

      # Fast burn: spending at 3x baseline in last hour
      - alert: AgentCostBudgetBurnRateFast
        expr: |
          rate(llm_cost_usd_total[1h])
          > 3 * avg_over_time(rate(llm_cost_usd_total[1h])[7d:1h])
        for: 15m
        labels:
          severity: page
        annotations:
          summary: "LLM cost burning at 3x baseline rate (1h window)"
          description: >
            Current hourly spend rate is {{ $value | humanize }} USD/s,
            which is 3x the 7-day average. Investigate for runaway agent loops.

      # Slow burn: 50% above baseline sustained for 6 hours
      - alert: AgentCostBudgetBurnRateSlow
        expr: |
          rate(llm_cost_usd_total[6h])
          > 1.5 * avg_over_time(rate(llm_cost_usd_total[6h])[7d:6h])
        for: 30m
        labels:
          severity: warning
        annotations:
          summary: "LLM cost 50% above baseline (6h window)"

      # Per-tenant spike: any tenant costing 5x average
      - alert: PerTenantCostSpike
        expr: |
          sum by (tenant_id) (rate(llm_cost_usd_total[15m]))
          > 5 * avg(sum by (tenant_id) (rate(llm_cost_usd_total[15m])))
        for: 10m
        labels:
          severity: warning
        annotations:
          summary: "Tenant {{ $labels.tenant_id }} spending 5x average"

      # Prompt bloat: p95 prompt length growing week-over-week
      - alert: PromptLengthRegression
        expr: |
          histogram_quantile(0.95,
            sum by (le, agent_name) (
              rate(agent_prompt_length_tokens_bucket[1h])
            )
          )
          > 1.5 * histogram_quantile(0.95,
              sum by (le, agent_name) (
                rate(agent_prompt_length_tokens_bucket[1h] offset 7d)
              )
            )
        for: 2h
        labels:
          severity: warning
        annotations:
          summary: "Agent {{ $labels.agent_name }} p95 prompt length is 50% longer than last week"
Correlating Cost with Quality

The most powerful use of cost signals is to correlate them with quality signals. If you have an eval score (task success rate, human preference rating, hallucination rate) measured alongside cost, you can compute a
cost-quality efficiency ratio
: how much quality are you getting per dollar spent? This enables systematic model routing decisions.

"""cost_quality_analysis.py

Analyses the cost-quality tradeoff across model variants.
Assumes session-level data is joined from metrics and eval store.
"""
from dataclasses import dataclass


@dataclass
class SessionRecord:
    session_id: str
    model: str
    agent_name: str
    input_tokens: int
    output_tokens: int
    cost_usd: float
    eval_score: float   # 0-1, higher is better
    succeeded: bool


def compute_cost_quality_report(sessions: list[SessionRecord]) -> dict:
    """Compute cost-quality metrics grouped by model."""
    from collections import defaultdict
    groups: dict[str, list[SessionRecord]] = defaultdict(list)
    for s in sessions:
        groups[s.model].append(s)

    report = {}
    for model, recs in groups.items():
        total_cost = sum(r.cost_usd for r in recs)
        n_sessions = len(recs)
        n_success = sum(1 for r in recs if r.succeeded)
        avg_eval = sum(r.eval_score for r in recs) / n_sessions if n_sessions else 0
        cost_per_session = total_cost / n_sessions if n_sessions else 0
        cost_per_success = total_cost / n_success if n_success else float("inf")
        # Quality per dollar: higher is better
        quality_per_dollar = avg_eval / cost_per_session if cost_per_session else 0

        report[model] = {
            "sessions": n_sessions,
            "success_rate": n_success / n_sessions if n_sessions else 0,
            "avg_eval_score": round(avg_eval, 4),
            "cost_per_session_usd": round(cost_per_session, 6),
            "cost_per_success_usd": round(cost_per_success, 6),
            "quality_per_dollar": round(quality_per_dollar, 4),
        }
    return report


# Example output interpretation:
# model A: quality_per_dollar = 42.0  (cheap + high quality)
# model B: quality_per_dollar = 18.0  (expensive + similar quality)
# => route to model A unless model B's higher absolute quality is necessary
When to Use It
From the first deployment of any agentic system that calls a paid LLM API. Cost signals have zero downside and immediate upside for budget visibility.
Before scaling any agent feature beyond a small pilot. Know the cost per session at low traffic before extrapolating to production load.
When running A/B tests on models or prompt strategies. Cost attribution by feature flag enables direct cost-quality tradeoff comparison.
In multi-tenant platforms where individual tenants should be accountable for their consumption.
When debugging runaway costs from infinite loops, recursive tool call chains, or unexpectedly large context accumulation.
Real-World Example

Consider an agentic code review assistant. In testing, each session costs roughly $0.03 because code snippets are short. After launch, a new feature allows users to review entire repositories: session cost spikes to $3.00 — a 100x increase. Without a cost burn rate alert, this is invisible until the monthly bill arrives. With a 3x burn rate alert on the hourly spending rate, the on-call engineer receives a page within 15 minutes of the first high-cost sessions completing. The alert's annotation links to a Grafana dashboard showing cost broken down by agent_name and feature_flag, immediately identifying the new repository-review feature as the source. The team can either add a token budget cap to the feature or roll it back while a cost optimisation is engineered.
Common Mistakes

Using the same label cardinality for cost metrics as for trace spans. Prometheus cost metrics with per-session-id labels will create millions of time series. Keep attribution at agent, tenant, and feature level — not request level.

Hardcoding prices in application code without a configuration mechanism. When the provider changes pricing, every deployed instance needs updating. Manage prices in a config file or environment variable.
Alerting only on total cost, not on per-agent or per-tenant cost. A tenant that doubles in cost is invisible in total-cost alerts if other tenants are stable.
Not tracking cache read tokens separately. Prompt caching can reduce effective cost by 50–90% for repetitive system prompts, but you cannot validate caching effectiveness without a dedicated metric.

Treating cost signals as the output of the billing department rather than engineering. Cost signals should be owned by the platform engineering team, be on the same dashboards as latency and error rate, and be part of the incident response runbook.
Best Practices
Emit cost metrics at the point of receiving each LLM API response, not in a separate billing pipeline. Real-time cost visibility requires real-time collection.
Track input and output tokens as separate counters. Input and output have different prices and different optimisation levers.
Track cache read tokens to validate prompt caching effectiveness. Without this metric, caching investment is invisible.
Set per-session and per-agent cost budgets in code (not just in dashboards) so runaway sessions are hard-capped before they can drain the account.
Correlate cost metrics with quality metrics at session granularity, stored in a data warehouse. Real-time Prometheus is for alerting; time-series warehouse data is for cost-quality analysis.
Review prompt length trend (p95 agent_prompt_length_tokens) weekly. Growing p95 prompt length is the earliest indicator of system prompt bloat before it becomes a cost problem.
Performance & Scalability

Cost metric collection adds negligible overhead because it executes synchronously after an already-completed LLM API call. The Prometheus counter increment is microseconds. The cardinality concern is label design: with three attribution dimensions (agent_name, tenant_id, feature_flag), and say 10 agents, 1,000 tenants, and 5 feature flags, the total series count is 10 × 1,000 × 5 = 50,000 series — acceptable for Prometheus. Adding a fourth high-cardinality dimension (e.g., model × region × prompt_version) can push this to millions. Use a recording rule to pre-aggregate to two dimensions in Prometheus, and push the four-dimensional raw data to a long-term warehouse (BigQuery, ClickHouse) for offline analysis.
Tooling & Ecosystem
Prometheus + Grafana — primary cost dashboard and alerting. Grafana's stat panels make cost/hour and cost/session visible at a glance on a golden signal dashboard.
Langfuse — open-source LLM observability platform with built-in cost tracking per trace and per project. Aggregates cost per tag, user, and session automatically.
Helicone — LLM proxy that intercepts API calls and records cost, latency, and token usage without SDK changes.
OpenMeter — usage metering infrastructure for per-tenant cost attribution with billing integration.
ClickHouse / BigQuery — columnar stores for long-term cost-quality correlation analysis. Ideal for the aggregated session-level records that combine token usage with eval scores.
Comparison with Alternatives
Approach
Real-time Cost Visibility
Per-tenant Attribution
Quality Correlation
Monthly provider invoice
No (monthly lag)
None
None
LLM proxy (Helicone)
Yes (per-request)
Via custom headers
Requires external eval data
Prometheus metrics (this approach)
Yes (15s scrape interval)
Yes (label-based)
Via warehouse join
Langfuse traces
Yes (per-trace)
Yes (user/session tags)
Yes (built-in eval integration)
Security & Reliability

Cost metrics contain cost data, not PII — but tenant_id labels are sensitive. Ensure cost dashboards have appropriate access controls so competitors or internal teams cannot infer each other's consumption patterns.

Implement hard per-session token budget caps in code as a reliability control, independent of alerting. A bug in a multi-hop agent that causes an infinite tool call loop can accrue thousands of dollars in minutes without a code-level cap.
Store pricing configuration in a version-controlled file, not hardcoded constants. When the provider changes pricing, a PR to update the config file is safer than a scattered code search.

Validate that cost metrics are non-zero after every deployment that touches LLM call paths. A zero-cost metric after a deployment indicates the instrumentation path has been broken and you have lost visibility.
Mental Model

Think of LLM cost as a fifth golden signal alongside latency, traffic, errors, and saturation — one that the original four-signal framework predates and therefore does not include. In the context of agentic AI, it is often the signal with the most direct business impact. A 10x cost spike that does not cause latency degradation or error increase is invisible to the standard golden signals but is acutely visible to the finance team and to budget alarms. By instrumenting cost with the same discipline as the other four signals — histograms, counters, alerting rules, SLOs — you give the cost signal the operational prominence it deserves alongside reliability metrics.
Key Takeaways
Token usage and cost are first-class operational signals that must be measured, alerted on, and attributed — not left to monthly billing.
Track input tokens, output tokens, and cache read tokens as separate Prometheus counters with attribution labels.
Derive cost_usd at collection time by multiplying token counts by the per-token model price. Store pricing in a config file, not hardcoded constants.
Use multi-window burn rate alerts (1h fast burn, 6h slow burn) to detect both cost spikes and gradual spend drift.
Attribute costs by agent, tenant, and feature flag. Per-tenant spike alerts catch runaway loops in individual accounts before they affect the whole platform.
Correlate cost with quality (eval score, success rate) to compute cost-per-outcome and make data-driven model routing decisions.
Implement hard per-session token budget caps in code as a reliability safeguard independent of alerting.
